main_summary.summary: William Saunders (born New York City, 1935; raised on Johns Island, South Carolina) recounts a six-decade trajectory from Sea Island farm childhood to lead organizer of the 1969 Charleston hospital workers' strike. Sent south at eighteen months, raised by grandparents in a Gullah farming community, he came to political consciousness through Esau Jenkins, the Progressive Club, and the District 9 school-board struggle. After volunteering for the U.S. Army at sixteen, he served in the Korean War, was wounded by a booby-trap mine on December 26, 1951, and returned home embittered by Army segregation and the Greyhound station encounter in Columbia. He attended a 1959 SCLC-Highlander Research Center session in Monteagle, Tennessee and was present during the raid that arrested Septima Clark. He rejected the nonviolent stance, identifying with Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X while continuing to provide support work, paperwork, food, mimeographed newsletters, through the Progressive Club. From his mattress-factory job he ran the Low Country Newsletter (built around equipment from Dr. Howard Levy and the American Friends Service Committee), helped organize the 1969 Medical College Hospital strike alongside Otis Robinson, and conducted back-channel negotiations with vice-president William Huffitz that brought trustees and worker leadership together. He details Governor Robert E. McNair's attempt to use him to deceive striking workers, SLED Chief J.P. "Pete" Strom's warning of an assassination plot, and Hugh C. Lane Sr.'s role in cutting HEW funding to force resolution. The interview also covers his 1980 state-senate run, NAACP opposition, and founding the Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program.
key_themes: Korean War service and racism in the U.S. military
class analysis of the civil rights movement
main_summary.historical_significance:
Saunders's testimony provides a first-person account of the 1969 Charleston hospital workers' strike from inside the local organizing apparatus, distinct from the SCLC and 1199 union narratives that have dominated the historiography. He claims the slogan 'I AM SOMEBODY' originated with local Black Muslim leader Otis Robinson at a 200-person worker meeting on the night of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination, not with Jesse Jackson's later PUSH-era popularization. He documents back-channel negotiations through Medical College vice-president William Huffitz, Hugh C. Lane Sr.'s intervention with the Nixon Administration to cut HEW funding to the hospital, and Governor Robert E. McNair's attempted deception that would have used Saunders to falsely announce a settlement to congregations at Morris Brown AME and Emanuel AME.

The interview also documents a Sea Islands organizing lineage running through Esau Jenkins, the Progressive Club, the 1959 Highlander raid that arrested Septima Clark, and Saunders's own rejection of nonviolence as a philosophy. His class-rather-than-race framework, formed through the Highlander encounter with poor Appalachian whites, and his account of NAACP opposition to his 1980 state-senate candidacy in favor of I. DeQuincey Newman supplies a counter-narrative to the conventional civil rights leadership account.

chapters.title: Picked Up With a Tag
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Kerry Taylor at North Charleston in June 2011, William Saunders recounts being born in New York City in 1935 and sent at eighteen months on a train, wearing a tag, to be picked up by his grandfather on Johns Island, South Carolina, where his mother's parents raised him apart from his siblings.

chapters.title: Can to Can't
chapters.summary: Saunders describes Johns Island as a paradise of subsistence where families ate from the river and woods and everyone had access to land, his self-taught grandfather who surveyed acres and planted boundary trees still standing, and how hand-me-down shoes, a roadside burn, and selling lightwood and his grandmother's chickens at five dollars apiece first brought him to the Charleston market.

chapters.title: They Just Stopped Having Elections
chapters.topic: Esau Jenkins and the School Board
chapters.summary: Saunders recalls knowing Esau Jenkins from childhood church and Jenkins's bus contracts for Black students sent to Burke High in Charleston, how Charleston County simply stopped holding School Board elections from 1956 to 1970 to keep Black candidates off, and how, after Stokely Carmichael came in 1967 and Saunders demanded three Black members on the District 9 board, the county appointed Jenkins to set the King-aligned founder against the Carmichael-aligned Saunders.

chapters.title: They Let My Age Be Eighteen
chapters.topic: The Army and the Korean War
chapters.summary: Saunders describes the teasing he endured as a poorly dressed island student and how at sixteen he and classmates volunteered for the Army, his age recorded as eighteen, trained in Hawaii and shipped to the Korean War, where he says he suffered more racism than anywhere in his life, with four Black soldiers in his company just after the 1951 integration and a first sergeant who held a forty-five on him on the front line.

chapters.title: You Don't Belong in Here
chapters.summary: Saunders recounts returning as a wounded hero yet riding segregated train coaches, being confronted at the Columbia Greyhound station by a policeman who told him he did not belong while his white Army buddies stayed silent, and describes stepping on a booby-trap trip flare on December 26, 1951 as the last man in a squad's single track through deep snow, then being sent back to the Korean front after four months in a Japanese hospital.

chapters.title: This Nonviolent Thing Is Not for Me
chapters.topic: Voter Teaching and the Highlander Raid
chapters.summary: Saunders recounts becoming a company clerk and sergeant at Fort Jackson, returning to Johns Island to teach people the constitutional passages required to register, some memorizing the whole text, his work as business manager of the Progressive Club and Esau Jenkins's adult school, and his 1959 trip to the Highlander Research Center where a police raid arrested Septima Clark on a beer pretext and convinced him nonviolence was not for him.

chapters.title: A Class System, Not a Race System
chapters.summary: Saunders recounts returning to Harlem in 1959 and explains that the Highlander gatherings brought Southerners together, including poor Appalachian whites facing strip mining, leading him to see the country as a class system in which race sits at the bottom, and recalls director Myles Horton's lesson that you help people get started and then let them decide what is best.

chapters.title: I Only Had One Cheek
chapters.summary: He clarifies that he did not oppose nonviolence so much as the idea of letting people beat him for nothing, did all the supportive paperwork, food, and background work for the movement without marching, and recalls Fannie Lou Hamer and others staying on Johns Island, Hamer cursing to avoid being beaten while demonstrating.

chapters.title: Lazy People Find an Easier Way
chapters.summary: Saunders recounts working twenty years in a mattress factory, a job he chose because activism could not get him fired, where his boss Julius Walsh made him foreman and gave him a private telephone for movement calls, and how, jailed repeatedly on pretexts, he founded the Low Country Newsletter with jailed Army doctor Howard Levy, using an American Friends Service Committee press to publish photo comparisons of the unequal Black and white island high schools.

chapters.title: American-aided Integration
chapters.summary: Saunders explains how Charleston leaders defeated Black power on the schools by stacking a consolidated nine-member board with at-large voting atop the existing eight boards, controlling all money and teachers, and why, contrasting the NAACP's interest in sending a few elite Black children to white schools with King's demand of good education for everyone, he favored the first desegregation ruling but fought the busing order, making enemies of Charleston's Black leaders.

chapters.title: They Asked the Governor Not to Certify Me
chapters.summary: He recounts winning election to the Office of Economic Opportunity regional board in 1967 only to have Charleston's Black leadership and NAACP petition the governor against certifying him, and how a Georgia official, Henrietta Canty, froze a million dollars for the county until they relented, after which the governor simply stopped certifying anyone so Saunders could serve.

chapters.title: Human Rights Before Civil Rights
chapters.summary: Saunders frames his work as about human rights rather than civil rights, and describes how the Low Country Newsletter exposed conditions for Black workers at the medical college, including the state's refusal to pay minimum wage, and how he helped five Black nurses fired for refusing improper duties get reinstated through Reginald Barrett of the civil rights commission.

chapters.title: Bring Six, Then Bring Twelve
chapters.summary: He recounts organizing the reinstated nurses by asking each to bring more people week after week until the group of three hundred outgrew Barrett's house and moved to the union hall, the workers seeking money but also respect and job classifications in a hospital where Black staff ate in the boiler room and pay depended on a supervisor's favor.

chapters.title: You Are Somebody
chapters.summary: Saunders credits Black Muslim leader Otis Robinson, echoed by Jesse Jackson, with the I-am-somebody affirmation telling hospital workers who fed and cleaned patients around the clock that they mattered, insists the union-hall meetings were a full community effort of women and men, recounts turning a windowless cell into singing the first night he was arrested, and describes how the few white workers who joined were exposed by Black spies and lost their jobs.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: Guards Who Took Over the Jail
chapters.summary: Saunders recounts how Local 1199 out of New York and SCLC, including Andrew Young, joined the organized workers in January 1969, and how he recruited young Black men, some previously in trouble, as guards who deliberately got arrested to protect jailed workers from inside, many later returning to school, one becoming a minister.

chapters.title: Skip the One They Fight Over
chapters.summary: He says the strike first got Black people elected to public office in South Carolina, and describes meeting secretly during the curfew with a medical university vice president to relay positions between the trustees and the union and Andrew Young, arranging a private leadership meeting where, of ten demands, he set aside the ones the trustees resisted and won agreement on eight, framing himself as the man who builds the stage rather than the star who plays on it.

chapters.title: We Won't Have a Riot, We'll Have a War
chapters.summary: He says his only quarrel with SCLC and the union was their failure to train the worker leaders to run the local afterward, not nonviolence, and recounts warning the FBI that Charleston would never riot or burn its own community but would wage a war if pushed, since the people were equipped to fight and only wanted fair treatment.

chapters.title: I Looked in the Trunk a Good While
chapters.summary: Saunders tells of the state law enforcement chief summoning him to a motel lined with police, where Saunders defiantly stood at his open car trunk before learning the chief wanted to offer protection against a plot on his life, and how he told him not to save him, citing King and Kennedy, since he and other Black men kept their own offensive apparatus for self-defense.

chapters.title: Why Don't You Send Me a Telegram
chapters.summary: He recounts Governor Robert McNair asking him to tell congregations at two AME churches that the strike would end the next day, Saunders sensing a trap and demanding a telegram the governor could not provide, then going home to Johns Island while roughly two hundred people were arrested at the church, refusing to be the man blamed for telling a lie.

chapters.title: Cut Off the Money
chapters.summary: Saunders explains that banker Hugh Lane, asked through the Nixon administration how to end the strike, advised cutting off the roughly seventy-five million dollars of federal HEW money to the medical college, and laments that SCLC and the union, not the local people who built the stage and took the beatings, received all the credit while Andrew Young's career rose.

chapters.part: A Public Life and Its Costs
chapters.title: I'm Going to Give You Access
chapters.summary: He calls the settlement nearly nothing but credits the strike with creating the South Carolina Human Affairs Commission and electing the first Black members to City Council, the County Council, and the legislature, and reflects on raising ten children to whom he gave the access he never had, refusing to let a hard childhood excuse hurting others.

chapters.part: A Public Life and Its Costs
chapters.title: They Switched In Mean Black Guys
chapters.summary: Saunders recounts moving from the hospital strike straight into the sanitation strike, targeting state institutions so a win would bind the whole state to minimum wage and training, and notes the bitter irony that after Black workers won the right to drive and ride up front, the system replaced mean white supervisors with mean Black ones who mistreated the workers worse.

chapters.part: A Public Life and Its Costs
chapters.title: They Wanted Their Own First Senator
chapters.summary: He recounts his 1980 run for state senate, winning the Democratic primary and runoff before the NAACP brought in outsiders to oppose him over his humble family background, having already arranged for I. DeQuincey Newman to become the first Black state senator, and attributes his close loss partly to Ronald Reagan drawing white voters to a straight ticket and to opponents who, like Americans who love politicians who lie, promised a bridge no freshman senator could deliver.

chapters.part: A Public Life and Its Costs
chapters.title: Nobody Could Change the Station
chapters.summary: Saunders reflects on the bitterness of a life lived without the right family background, then describes building a number-one-rated daytime radio station out of about twenty-seven by serving the public with gospel, talk, and jazz, listeners so loyal they removed the tuning knob, and laments deregulation under Reagan that ended public-service requirements.

chapters.part: A Public Life and Its Costs
chapters.title: Making Robots Out of Young People
chapters.summary: He worries that teaching children to depend on gadgets rather than think is making robots of them, recounts getting Gullah recognized as a language around 1969 or 1970, and describes a Johns Island concerned-citizens group fighting a road project where former Klansmen and farmers sit together by addressing only right and wrong, never religion or politics.

chapters.part: What He Learned and What He Fears
chapters.title: Never Been Indicted
chapters.summary: Saunders names his proudest achievement at seventy-six as never having been indicted by a government that gets rid of people like him, says no one taught him to organize because losing was his greatest teacher, recounts an algebra teacher who marked his correct answers wrong for using his own formula until the principal vindicated him, and teaches that people must enjoy the process rather than chase end results.

chapters.part: What He Learned and What He Fears
chapters.title: Still Run It Today
chapters.summary: Saunders says Korea enlightened him toward helping people, and recounts founding the Sea Island Sickle Cell Anemia Program around 1970, still running despite a seventy-five percent budget cut, in an area with the highest sickle cell rates in the country, while criticizing South Carolina's Republican leadership for opposing good education, health care, and equal justice.

chapters.part: What He Learned and What He Fears
chapters.title: We Got to Do Something About Tomorrow
chapters.summary: Saunders voices concern for the environment and worries that buying three or four politicians is enough to get any outcome, citing a county council that keeps voting on an island road until it wins, describes developers giving thousand-dollar gifts to children and churches to take over the groups that once helped people, and closes, drinking cherry wine and praying, by warning that the country must act for tomorrow or be lost across generations.

loc_video.caption: William Saunders oral history interview conducted by Kieran Walsh Taylor in Charleston, South Carolina, 2011 June 09
